ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 11713004 refers to water as a substance; no specific bottled-water product code exists in SNOMED CT. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 ml single-serve plastic bottle of commercially bottled water, equivalent to approximately 500 g.

nutrient_source.title: Water, bottled, generic — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for bottled water (generic) used. All macronutrients are confirmed zero or trace. Sodium reported as 5 mg per 100 g for typical mineral/bottled water; potassium 1 mg per 100 g. Values are consistent with standard bottled water composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water contains no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore has no measurable glycemic index. By convention, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is set to 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0. This convention is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database practice for zero-carbohydrate beverages.

hypertension.guidance: Bottled water contains approximately 5 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is nutritionally negligible and poses no concern for individuals managing hypertension; adequate daily water intake is in fact recommended to support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Bottled water contains no carbohydrates, sugars, or calories and therefore has no effect on blood glucose levels, making it the ideal hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, cholesterol, and sodium of clinical significance
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bottled water contains no fat, cholesterol, or clinically significant sodium, and adequate hydration supports cardiovascular function; it is universally recommended as the primary beverage for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bottled water (500 ml) is a commercially packaged, single-serve beverage consisting entirely of purified, mineral, or spring water with no added ingredients, calories, macronutrients, or artificial substances. It is sold in a sealed plastic or glass bottle and is widely available across Singapore and globally, including at quick-service restaurants such as Dunkin' Donuts. The product contains zero energy, zero protein, zero fat, zero carbohydrates, and zero sugar per 100 ml, with only trace minerals such as sodium (approximately 5 mg/100 ml), calcium (approximately 6 mg/100 ml), potassium (approximately 1 mg/100 ml), and magnesium (approximately 2 mg/100 ml) depending on the source water. It has no glycemic impact and is suitable for all dietary patterns including vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free. Clinically, bottled water is the safest and most universally recommended beverage for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, obesity, and chronic kidney disease. It supports hydration without contributing to caloric intake, blood glucose elevation, or cardiovascular risk. The 500 ml serving size is a standard single-serve portion commonly consumed with meals or as a standalone hydration choice throughout the day.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bottled water?
Is bottled water good for diabetics?
Can I drink bottled water if I have high blood pressure?
What is the nutritional value of water?
How much sodium is in bottled water?

confidence_reason: Bottled water is a well-characterised, zero-macronutrient product with values confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831). All core macros are confirmed zero by nature and by the approved source. Mineral trace values are consistent across multiple approved databases. No estimation was required for macronutrients.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero as bottled water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. All vitamin and micronutrient fields that are 0.0 are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for bottled water; no -1 sentinel values are used as the source explicitly reports these as zero or not detected. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0. The cuisine field from the input ('Coffee') reflects the field survey platform category tag for Dunkin' Donuts and is not a meaningful cuisine classification for water; cuisine has been corrected to 'Universal / Non-cuisine'. Serving size is 500 g (500 ml) as stated in the product name. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 (confirmed zero) per USDA source for bottled water.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Coffee' (field survey platform tag) to 'Universal / Non-cuisine' as water has no cuisine classification.
Set glycemic_index to 1 (sentinel value) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule; documented in notes and gi_source.
Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for all zero core macros per zero-macro justification rule.
Provided multilingual local_names and aliases in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Used USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved secondary source for generic bottled water.
Set all per_100g values to numeric; no -1 sentinel values used as USDA source explicitly reports all fields.
Documented all 0.0 values with 'naturally zero' or 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
